did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes and evaluates an explainable image classification framework using machine learning techniques (segmentation, clustering, feature extraction, and a linear white box prediction model) for skin cancer prediction. The study utilizes the ISIC dataset for skin cancer diagnosis tasks.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents a novel explainable image classification framework. It details original experimental methods, including segmentation, clustering, and feature extraction, and reports new results from applying this framework to skin cancer and plant disease prediction datasets (ISIC and PlantVillage) with specific performance metrics compared to state-of-the-art models.

title: A novel explainable image classification framework: case study on skin cancer and plant disease prediction
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel explainable image classification framework for skin cancer and plant disease prediction, addressing the lack of transparency in black-box deep learning models. The framework combines segmentation and clustering techniques to extract texture features from image subregions, followed by feature filtering to remove high correlations, and trains an intrinsic linear white box prediction model. The authors evaluated the model using the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset for skin cancer and the PlantVillage dataset for plant diseases. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed model achieved prediction accuracy within 1–2% of state-of-the-art black box convolutional neural networks. Additionally, the framework provides meaningful, global, and understandable explanations of its decision behavior through a hierarchy-based tree approach, ensuring reliability and trustworthiness for clinical and agricultural applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on skin cancer prediction using the ISIC dataset. The framework distinguishes between malignant and benign lesions, with high irregularity in the boundary and inner regions identified as signs of malignant cancer.
The study includes benign skin lesions in the ISIC dataset for classification. Low irregularity (smooth texture) is associated with benign lesions.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (malignant vs. benign skin cancer; specific plant disease types)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly proposes an 'explainable image classification framework' for 'skin cancer and plant disease prediction'. The task involves distinguishing between malignant and benign skin lesions using the ISIC dataset (balanced with 1400 benign and 1400 malignant instances) and classifying plant diseases using the PlantVillage dataset. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy, F1-score, Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, and NPV.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public access archive of skin images for teaching and testing automated diagnostic systems, acquired from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration. The study used a balanced subset created by randomly selecting instances from the initial imbalanced dataset.

use.training: Used for training the explainable image classification framework for skin cancer prediction.
use.validation: Used for validation of the model (via tenfold cross-validation).
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance (via tenfold cross-validation).

datasets_used.name: PlantVillage Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset acquired from Mohanty’s GitHub Repository containing various plant types (peppers, potatoes, and tomatoes) composed of balanced datasets of diseased and healthy subjects. The study created three classification tasks based on this dataset.
classes_used: Tomato (Healthy vs Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus)

use.training: Used for training the framework for plant disease prediction tasks.
use.validation: Used for validation of the model (via tenfold cross-validation).
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance (via tenfold cross-validation).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the proposed explainable image classification framework and compare it with state-of-the-art black box models on the ISIC and PlantVillage datasets.
To check for high correlation among extracted features and remove highly correlated features (threshold > 0.9) during the feature selection process.
To check for high correlation among extracted features as an alternative to Pearson correlation; no significant difference in performance was observed compared to Pearson.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, significance tests for comparing model performances). It utilized Pearson and Spearman correlation tests for feature selection to remove highly correlated features (threshold > 0.9), but these were not used for statistical hypothesis testing of the model's predictive performance.

architecture_used.main_model: TXM (Totally eXplainable Model), an intrinsic linear white box prediction model; PXM (Partially eXplainable Model) for comparison; Black Box CNNs (ResNet50, VGG16, MobileNetV2, NASNetMobile, InceptionV2, Xception, InceptionResNetV2, DenseNet201, EfficientNet3, EfficientNet5) used for comparison.
architecture_used.pretraining: The Black Box CNN models were pretrained on the ImageNet database. The proposed TXM and PXM models were trained from scratch using extracted features.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported for the proposed framework; input images were segmented into subregions.

framework_components: Segmentation techniques to identify image object regions.
Clustering techniques to divide regions into smaller subregions.
Feature extraction block computing transparent features (texture features like mean and standard deviation for TXM; co-occurrence and run length for PXM) from subregions.
Feature transformation-selection block applying Pearson/Spearman correlation tests to filter highly correlated features (threshold > 0.9).
Feature interpretation block computing feature contributions via a hierarchy-based tree approach.
architecture_details.model_type: Intrinsic linear white box prediction model (logistic regression implied by 'linear' and 'white box' context in explainable AI literature, though not explicitly named as such, it is described as 'linear prediction model').
architecture_details.feature_types_TXM: Simple texture features (mean and standard deviation) from segmented subregions.
architecture_details.feature_types_PXM: Complex texture features (co-occurrence, run length) from the whole image region.

training_details.learning_rate: Initial learning rate of 10^-4 for Black Box CNNs; reduced by a factor of 0.5 every 3 epochs if no validation improvement, down to a minimum of 10^-7.
training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation, flipping, and zoom in (for Black Box CNNs).
training_details.output_layer_BB: 512 neurons with ReLU activation, followed by a dropout component, and a final softmax activation function.

classification_or_output_head.TXM: Linear white box prediction model utilizing filtered texture features.
classification_or_output_head.PXM: Linear white box prediction model utilizing complex texture features.
classification_or_output_head.BB_models: Softmax activation function on final output neurons.

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: ResNet50 has over 23 million trainable parameters. Specific parameter counts for TXM, PXM, or other BB models were not explicitly reported in the provided text.

preprocessing_techniques: Segmentation using Otsu and Canny algorithms to extract Regions of Interest (ROI) and filter out noise.
Clustering using K-means algorithm to divide segmented regions into subregions (10 clusters for skin cancer, 15 for plant leaves).
Feature extraction from subregions including mean and standard deviation of pixel values for color channels (Red, Green, Blue).
Feature extraction of general characteristics: number of open loops, number of closed loops, and size of open/closed loops.
Feature filtering using Pearson and Spearman correlation tests to remove highly correlated features (threshold > 0.9).

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used as a common augmentation method for the Black Box CNN models to create a more robust and diverse input dataset.

conclusions: The proposed explainable image classification framework achieves prediction accuracy within 1–2% of state-of-the-art black box convolutional neural networks, demonstrating that intrinsic white box models can perform comparably to black box models.

The framework provides meaningful, global, and understandable explanations of its decision behavior through a hierarchy-based tree approach, making it trustworthy for clinical and agricultural applications.

The model ensures reliability and trustworthiness by combining segmentation and clustering techniques to extract texture features, followed by a feature filtering procedure that removes highly correlated features.

The study addresses the lack of transparency in black box deep learning models by proposing an intrinsic linear white box prediction model that exposes all features contributing to the prediction, unlike post-hoc methods which may not expose all contributing features.

The framework was successfully applied to both skin cancer prediction (using the ISIC dataset) and plant disease prediction (using the PlantVillage dataset), showing its generalizability across different domains.

The authors acknowledge that while black box models achieve remarkable performance, their lack of explainability and trust is a major drawback for real-world applications, particularly in critical fields like healthcare.
important_remarks: The study addresses class imbalance in the ISIC dataset by manually creating a balanced subset (1400 benign, 1400 malignant) via random selection, which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence.

The proposed white box model achieves accuracy within 1-2% of state-of-the-art black box CNNs, but this performance gap is small and may not justify the complexity of the feature engineering pipeline in all contexts.

The framework relies on segmentation (Otsu/Canny) and clustering (K-means) for feature extraction; performance is contingent on the quality of these preprocessing steps, which may fail on noisy or low-quality images.
Feature filtering removes features with Pearson/Spearman correlation > 0.9 to prevent collinearity, but this aggressive filtering might discard potentially informative but correlated features.
The model provides global explanations via a hierarchy-based tree, which applies to all instances rather than providing individualized local explanations for specific patient cases.

The study uses the PlantVillage dataset for external validation on plant diseases, demonstrating generalizability, but this dataset consists of controlled, high-quality images that differ significantly from clinical dermoscopic images.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to compare the proposed model against black box models; comparisons are based on point estimates of metrics.

The framework is designed as an intrinsic white box model to ensure transparency, contrasting with post-hoc methods like Grad-CAM which only highlight regions without exposing the full decision logic.
The study does not report external validation on independent clinical datasets or prospective trials, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical deployment readiness.

Data leakage risks are mitigated by using tenfold cross-validation, but the specific split details (e.g., patient-level vs. image-level splits) are not explicitly detailed in the provided context, which is critical for dermatological data.
